spec_match.rationale: Page exercises the concept interactively but stops short of touching the real API surface — the underlying spec behaviour is simulated rather than invoked.

interactive.rationale: Real interactive controls drive the page (5 buttons, 2 inputs, 2 selects, 0 ranges, 5 listeners) — well past the static-card bar.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Concept page lede restates and delivers on the framing used in the feature index card.

edge_cases.rationale: Only the happy path is demonstrated — no visible error / unsupported / boundary handling for the user to explore.

references.rationale: Page cites the required chromestatus.com/feature/5207202081800192 self-heal link (CLAUDE.md rule 3).

design_system.rationale: Uses CSS variables throughout, no raw hex of note, and button overrides keep background + color paired (CLAUDE.md rules 5 and 6).

openQuestions.title: Exercise more of the Full frame rate render blocking attribute spec surface
openQuestions.detail: The spec (see chromestatus) covers more than the page demonstrates. Walk back through the spec's exposed methods, properties, or modes and add controls for the ones that materially change behaviour. Today the page only hints at a subset.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate failure and edge-case branches
openQuestions.detail: Add visible branches for the unsupported / unavailable / error / boundary inputs paths. For built-in AI APIs, that means 'downloadable', 'downloading', 'unavailable' availability states; for HTTP features, error responses and CSP blocks; for CSS features, zero / extreme / inherited values. Today only the happy path is reachable from the UI.

openQuestions.title: Mirror the spec's stated motivation in the demo
openQuestions.detail: ChromeStatus motivation reads: 'The render blocking mechanism may be adopted to throttle the rendering. By adding a <link rel="expect" blocking="render"> element, the webpage can stop rendering until certain critical elements are fully parsed. However this will cause the ...'. The demo should make that motivation visible — show the problem the spec is solving (before/after, with-without, attacker view) so a reader leaves understanding why the feature ships, not just what it does.

summary: 'Loading Timeline Planner' misses a non-trivial part of the rubric (1 fail / 1 partial). The open questions list captures the highest-leverage fixes — usually adding a real interactive control or wiring the chromestatus reference link.
